entries.definition: Not willing to do something; hesitant.
entries.explanation: Used when someone agrees to do something but does not really want to, often because of doubt or fear. It describes an inner resistance rather than an open refusal.
entries.exampleBook: She was reluctant to leave the safety of the lighthouse, even as the storm calmed.
entries.exampleLearning: He was reluctant to admit his mistake in front of the whole team.
entries.grammar: Gradable adjective. Followed by 'to' + infinitive: reluctant to do something. Noun form takes 'reluctance to'.
entries.note: Often paired with 'to admit' — good for formal writing.

entries.definition: To think about something deeply and for a long time.
entries.explanation: Suggests slow, serious, often quiet thought about a plan, an idea, or a possibility. Stronger and more deliberate than 'consider'.
entries.exampleBook: He stood at the window, contemplating the grey sea.
entries.exampleLearning: Before changing careers, she spent months contemplating the risks.
entries.grammar: Transitive verb. Patterns: contemplate + noun, contemplate + -ing form (contemplate leaving). NOT followed by 'to' + infinitive.
entries.note: Remember: contemplate DOING, never contemplate TO DO.

entries.definition: Not obvious; delicate and difficult to notice.
entries.explanation: Describes something small, gentle, or clever that is easy to miss — a change, a flavor, a hint, a difference. The 'b' is silent.
entries.exampleBook: There was a subtle shift in his tone, so slight that only she noticed it.
entries.exampleLearning: The new design makes subtle improvements that you only notice after a while.
entries.grammar: Gradable adjective. Comparative: subtler (or more subtle), subtlest. Silent 'b'.

entries.definition: To do or complete a task, plan, or promise.
entries.explanation: A very common phrasal verb in academic and professional English, used for performing planned work: experiments, research, surveys, orders.
entries.exampleBook: Researchers carried out a series of interviews across five countries.
entries.exampleLearning: The team carried out a survey to verify the results.
entries.grammar: Transitive and separable: carry out a plan / carry a plan out. Passive is common: 'The study was carried out in 2024.'
entries.note: Preferred verb for experiments/research in academic writing.

entries.explanation: A formal word found in legal, academic, and literary writing. Unusually, it can come before or after the noun: 'notwithstanding the delay' or 'the delay notwithstanding'.
entries.exampleBook: Notwithstanding the objections of the board, the plan proceeded.
entries.exampleLearning: Her inexperience notwithstanding, she handled the negotiation brilliantly.
entries.grammar: Preposition (can follow its object), also adverb. Register: formal/legal.
entries.wordForms: functions as preposition, adverb, and (rarely) conjunction
entries.note: Impressive in formal writing, but don't overuse it.
